summary: Gruber examines the state of Catalyst apps two months after launch, finding them consistently subpar as Mac experiences. Using Twitter's Catalyst app as a case study, he notes that even basic Mac conventions like Page Up/Down key support were missing at launch. He argues the problem is twofold: Catalyst itself isn't a full-fledged Mac framework yet, and iOS's culture of nonstandard custom UI creates apps that look and feel wrong when brought to the Mac. He concludes that even standard UIKit iPad apps are a questionable starting point for Mac apps, and nonstandard ones are a terrible starting point, undermining Catalyst's 'just click a checkbox' promise.
key_points: Catalyst apps almost universally look, feel, and work wrong on the Mac, even from major developers like Twitter
iOS's single-app paradigm tolerates nonstandard UI that becomes glaringly incongruous when apps must coexist side-by-side on the Mac desktop
The Catalyst framework itself is immature, but the deeper problem is that iOS apps using custom controls instead of standard UIKit don't get automatic Mac convention support
The amount of work needed to make nonstandard iPad apps into good Mac apps defeats Catalyst's entire 'just click a checkbox' premise
Mac ease of use depends on cohesion between apps sharing the same shortcuts, controls, and windowing behavior — a fundamentally different model from iOS
money_quotes: I'm just not seeing it with Catalyst apps. They almost all look and feel and work wrong.
It's kind of crazy that support for those keys wasn't there from the start. 15 years ago you'd almost never find a Mac app that didn't support them.
I think part of the problem is Catalyst itself — it just doesn't feel like nearly a full-fledged framework for creating proper Mac apps yet.
Try moving these apps to the Mac and the nonstandard UIs stick out like a sore thumb.
I remain unconvinced that standard UIKit iPad apps are a good starting point for good Mac apps.
It's all just typing — but it's so much work it seems to defeat the entire 'Just click a checkbox in Xcode' premise and promise of Catalyst.
That innocuous diversity becomes incongruous once those same apps have to live side-by-side.
key_insight: Catalyst's fundamental flaw is that iOS's tolerance for nonstandard custom UI, enabled by its single-app paradigm, produces apps that are incoherent when forced to coexist on the Mac desktop where cohesion between applications is essential.
